headlines.title: South Korean central bank raises rates as economy shows strength

ripple_effect.name: Central bank interest rate hikes

historical_precedents.event: U.S. Federal Reserve's fastest rate-hiking cycle in decades
historical_precedents.what_happened: The Fed raised its benchmark interest rate from near zero to over 5% in about 16 months, responding to the highest inflation in roughly 40 years.
historical_precedents.market_impact: The average 30-year U.S. mortgage rate roughly doubled from around 3% to over 7%, home sales slowed sharply, and high-growth technology stocks — more sensitive to rate changes — fell substantially more than the broader market in 2022.

historical_precedents.event: Fed Chair Paul Volcker's rate hikes to break double-digit inflation
historical_precedents.what_happened: The Fed raised interest rates to nearly 20% to bring runaway inflation under control.
historical_precedents.market_impact: The policy triggered a deep recession and a sharp rise in unemployment before inflation was brought down over the following years.

possibility_chains: Higher central bank interest rates have historically raised borrowing costs across the economy — mortgages, auto loans, and business credit — with mortgage and housing markets typically among the most sensitive.

Rate hikes have historically pressured stock valuations, particularly for growth-oriented companies whose profits are expected further in the future, since higher rates reduce the present value of those future earnings.
Aggressive rate-hiking cycles have historically raised the risk of an economic slowdown or recession, though the size and timing of that effect has varied a great deal across different hiking cycles.

headlines.title: Oil pipelines around the Strait of Hormuz won't end the threat Iran poses to Middle East crude exports

ripple_effect.name: Persian Gulf oil supply shock

historical_precedents.what_happened: Iran and Iraq attacked oil tankers transiting the Persian Gulf and Strait of Hormuz, and Iran repeatedly threatened to close the strait.
historical_precedents.market_impact: Shipping insurance war-risk premiums in the Gulf rose sharply, and the U.S. Navy began escorting reflagged Kuwaiti tankers in 1987 to keep the strait open.

historical_precedents.event: Drone and missile attack on Saudi Aramco's Abqaiq and Khurais facilities
historical_precedents.what_happened: The attack (which Saudi Arabia and the U.S. blamed on Iran, a claim Iran denied) temporarily knocked out roughly half of Saudi Arabia's oil output, about 5% of global supply.
historical_precedents.market_impact: Brent crude jumped about 15% in a single day — the largest one-day oil price increase since the 1990–91 Gulf War.

historical_precedents.what_happened: Iraq's invasion of Kuwait removed a large share of Gulf oil exports from the market and raised fears of a wider regional war.
historical_precedents.market_impact: Oil prices roughly doubled in the months following the invasion before falling back once the war ended and supply concerns eased.

possibility_chains: Roughly a fifth of the world's oil is estimated to pass through the Strait of Hormuz, so a sustained blockage or major disruption there has historically driven crude oil prices sharply higher.

Because ammonia-based fertilizer production is natural-gas intensive, and natural gas prices often move together with oil, a prolonged energy price spike has historically fed through into higher fertilizer costs over the following months.
Fertilizer is a major input cost in food production, so energy shocks of this kind have historically been followed — often with a lag — by higher food prices.
Shipping insurers have historically raised war-risk premiums for vessels transiting the Gulf during periods of heightened tension, adding to the landed cost of oil and other cargo.

headlines.title: Uganda: WHO Warns DR Congo Ebola Outbreak Is Outpacing Response As Uganda Reaches Milestone

ripple_effect.name: Disease outbreak with international health emergency

historical_precedents.what_happened: The World Health Organization declared a Public Health Emergency of International Concern as Ebola spread across Guinea, Liberia, and Sierra Leone; several airlines suspended routes to the region and some countries imposed travel restrictions.
historical_precedents.market_impact: The World Bank estimated the outbreak cost the three hardest-hit countries over $2 billion in lost economic output.

historical_precedents.what_happened: The WHO declared a Public Health Emergency as Zika spread across the Americas, linked to birth defects; several countries issued travel advisories affecting tourism, notably ahead of the Rio de Janeiro Olympics.
historical_precedents.market_impact: Tourism-dependent economies in affected regions reported measurable declines in visitor bookings during the advisory period.

possibility_chains: A WHO-declared public health emergency has historically triggered travel advisories and, in some cases, temporary route suspensions by airlines.
Disease outbreaks with international travel warnings have historically reduced tourism and business travel to the affected region for the duration of the advisory.
Outbreaks affecting agricultural or manufacturing regions have historically disrupted local labor supply and production.
Vaccine and treatment developers have historically seen increased investment and expedited regulatory review during major outbreaks.
